channel.title: Latest Updates
channel.description: The most recent blogs and online articles from The Economist

item.title: What to watch this week
item.description: Our recommendations send characters into the unknown

item.title: David Hockney believed in working from the heart
item.description: Britainâs boldest and most innovative painter died on June 11th, aged 88

item.title: Quid game: challenges for South Koreaâs president
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: Indiaâs cockroach party and remembering author Marjane Satrapi

item.title: The US in Brief: The spy who came in from the golf course
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: The amphibious villagers of Indonesia
item.description: As Java sinks into the sea, locals have been forced to live with water

item.title: Britainâs defence secretary falls on his sword
item.description: John Healey calls out a government that lacks seriousness when it comes to the countryâs security

item.title: Why Americaâs Treasury market needs a fix
item.description: Our podcast on markets, the economy and business. This week, is it too late to save the worldâs safe asset?

item.title: Economic data, commodities and markets
What âBackroomsâ and âObsessionâ reveal about Gen Zâs fears
item.description: Two new horror films have hit a nerve with youngsters

item.title: Marjane Satrapi set out to correct the Westâs views of Iran
item.description: The author of âPersepolisâ, an international sensation, died on June 4th, aged 56

item.title: Steven Spielberg has more to say about aliens
item.description: Returning to them in âDisclosure Dayâ, the great director raises the stakes

item.title: The people behind the largest art heist in history
item.description: A new book looks at the trade in masterpieces pilfered from Cambodia

item.title: The most hated countries at the FIFA World Cup
item.description: What national anthems reveal about old enemiesÂ

item.title: Another new boss aims to fix the worldâs biggest chocolate-maker
item.description: Will Hein Schumacherâs turnaround plan for Barry Callebaut work?

item.title: Too many people are shockingly bad at prioritisation
item.description: Choosing where to focus is among the most important skills

item.title: The worldâs wealthy are migrating like never before
item.description: A booming industry of advisers is easing their passage

item.title: In China ride-hailing work is a last resort for rural labourers
item.description: But now Shenzhen says it has too many of them

item.title: Chinaâs notorious university-entrance exam is changing
item.description: Demography and AI are set to transform the gaokao

item.title: Japan is rethinking its divorce laws
item.description: A move to introduce joint custody marks a big shift

item.title: Techno-libertarians are flocking to the Caribbean
item.description: Angry locals are trying to shut them down

item.title: Fighting in Mogadishu risks making a weak state weaker
item.description: A presidential power grab has convulsed Somali politics

item.title: Could Eritrea come in from the cold?
item.description: Isaias Afwerki, its reclusive dictator, has spotted an opening to reconcile with America

item.title: The first-ever robotic rescue at sea is a milestone
item.description: An American drone recovers downed pilots in the Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Iran has lost its fear of war
item.description: A once-cautious regime is making a risky bet on low-level conflict

item.title: Tik-Tocqueville
item.description: Why âDemocracy in Americaâ is worth going back to on the countryâs 250th birthday

item.title: Social media is behind both âteen takeoversâ and the outrage they fuel
item.description: Cities struggle to respond to big gatherings of young folk

item.title: Americaâs quintessential places are getting old, fast
item.description: Welcome to the greying suburbs

item.title: Ukraine is transplanting its industrial heart to the west
item.description: Soviet-built factory towns are in the wrong spot now that Russia is the enemy

item.title: Britainâs rail nationalisation is going full steam ahead
item.description: But it risks going down the wrong track

item.title: A kidsâ social-media ban would be a bad parting gift from Keir Starmer
item.description: But it perfectly captures the British prime ministerâs vanilla brand of populism

item.title: Silicon Valley needs to get God
item.description: Tech must reorient towards moral purpose for itâand humankindâto flourish, argues Glen Weyl

item.title: What can you learn from the Premier League?
item.description: Also this week, school meals in Indonesia, commuting, Barney Frank, MAGA tax, condoms

item.title: The best way to celebrate America at 250 is to get behind the wheel
item.description: Preferably with our new podcast series for company

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Business
Politics
2. Against all obstacles
item.description: A group of hardened criminals unpack the American dream

item.title: 1. Game of chance
item.description: In 1831, America looked like the future of democracy. Is that still true today?

item.title: Can Indiaâs cockroach party become a political movement?
item.description: The government is betting that the answer is no

item.title: Asian activists say too much egg production is cruel
item.description: They are hatching ingenious schemes

item.title: How to make the beautiful game exciting again
item.description: As the World Cup gets under way, readers send in their thoughts

item.title: How big are Chinaâs emerging industries?
item.description: Probably not big enough to offset the drag from the old

item.title: The US in Brief: âI love the inflationâ
item.description: Our daily political update, featuring the stories that matter

item.title: Checks and Balance newsletter: The year America reckoned with AIDS
item.description: Jon Fasman, our senior culture correspondent, on how activists forced the country to confront the crisis

item.title: Kicking and screaming: protests at World Cup
item.description: Also on the daily podcast: how to tax space and angry Indian elites

item.title: For its own sake, China should change its growth model
item.description: It is suffering economic costs for its industrial dominanceÂ

item.title: A trade war between the EU and China seems inevitable
item.description: Europe sees Chinese subsidies, China sees European weakness

item.title: Stears wants to be Africaâs Bloomberg terminal
item.description: As the continentâs markets mature, high-quality financial analysis is more essential than ever

item.title: The worldâs strategic oil reserves are running out fast
item.description: They cannot hold back the energy crunch for ever

item.title: How to share AI riches
item.description: From Donald Trump to Sam Altman, the idea of redistributing them is catching on. Does it make sense?

item.title: Ukraineâs war is now longer than the first world war
item.description: The ghost of Versailles haunts Ukraineâs peace

item.title: A frenzied knife attack by a refugee has put Northern Ireland on edge
item.description: It set off destructive riots in Belfast and beyond

item.title: The Knicks represent New Yorkâand capitalismâat its best
item.description: That is why New Yorkers love them

item.title: Entertainment is being deglobalised
item.description: Why monoculture events like the World Cup are becoming the exception

item.title: How to fix football
item.description: A handpicked article read aloud from the latest issue of The Economist

item.title: Syria is an unexpected beneficiary of the Gulf war
item.description: The revival of an old oil-export route from Iraq to the Mediterranean helps Syriaâs new regime

item.title: How to win the World Cup
item.description: Being rich helps, but being open to immigration works best of all

item.title: American capitalism is run by millionaires, not billionaires
item.description: They hide in plain sightâand wield enormous power

item.title: New techniques can predict and prevent lung cancer
item.description: A molecular signature can identify those most at risk

item.title: The World Cup has always been beset by scandal and strife
item.description: So has FIFA, the outfit that administers it

item.title: The World Cup paradox
item.description: How the rules of both entertainment and soft power are being rewritten

item.title: Too much Chinese science is ignored by the West
item.description: A bad reputation and cultural ignorance are probably responsible

item.title: Donald Trumpâs least bad option in Iran
item.description: He must swallow his pride and accept a deal worse than the pre-war status quo

item.title: Americaâs mayors join the scrabble to become influencers
item.description: A dispatch from Long Beach, California

item.title: Eat your electronics, boost your health
item.description: Our podcast on science and technology. This week, why swallowing devices could soon support your health

item.title: The Green Party responds to our leader arguing that the new leftâs policies threaten prosperity
item.description: The green economy creates thousands of jobs and strengthens energy security, argues Sarah Wakefield

item.title: British politicians are racing to the hard right
item.description: Amid riots in Belfast, Restore promises to put âmurderous third-world savages to deathâ

item.title: From morning in America to endless conflict
Fear of the SaaSpocalypse is tormenting techland
item.description: Software once ate the world. Now it is in danger of eating itself

item.title: President Prabowo Subianto responds to our recent briefing
item.description: We need to work for our common future and deliver on the platform on which we were elected, he says

item.description: Is that a rocket in your lobby, or are you just happy to see me?Â

item.description: The alliance is in trouble, but Erdogan is enthusiastic

item.title: Ukrainian strikes are inflicting pain deep inside Russia
item.description: Our modelling suggests they are doing even more damage than commonly assumed

item.title: Why strongmen are wrong to loathe Europe
item.description: The leaders of America, China and Russia scorn consensual politics. That is a mistake

item.description: For some Americans, the country has become a cautionary tale, writes Georgia Banjo, our Britain correspondent

item.description: Peru, looking for its ninth president in a decade, is in a tight spot

item.description: Kevin Warsh, the unlucky new chairman, has seen his case for lower interest rates disintegrate

item.description: The iPhone-maker does not need to build models to cash in on the technology

item.description: An effort to build a plane between Britain, Italy and Japan risks similar problems to one by France, Germany and Spain

item.description: More involvement from banks and crypto firms in the Treasury market is a good thing. Neither will outrun immense issuance

item.description: As interest from other buyers dwindles, the trade in Treasuries increasingly relies on risky investors
